---
title: "23. AI as Adversarial Reviewer"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb09_attack_the_analysis_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** When an adversarial reviewer hands you a list of
> flaws in your own analysis, you decide which flaws are real by running the single
> data check that confirms or refutes each, instead of acting on whichever reviewer
> sounds most certain. This chapter equips you to pre-commit your robustness checks,
> verify every flag against your data, and defend the one bounded claim that
> survives.

## Why this decision matters

Picture the review of a pull request that claims a speedup. A **staff engineer** is
a senior developer whose sign-off lets your change ship to real users, and who
answers for it when production tells a different story. Example: the engineer who
approved your caching change is the one paged when latency climbs at 2 a.m. Their
concern is blunt.

> *"Do not tell me the numbers got better. Tell me which check you ran that would
> have caught it if they hadn't. I trust the check, not the graph."*

A confident benchmark, a fluent AI critique, and a clean-looking table can all be
wrong in the same convincing way. The engineer is asking for what sounds least
impressive and matters most: the check you committed to before you looked, and the
flag you actually verified against the data.

## The concept

An **adversarial reviewer** is a reader whose job is to attack your result and find
where it breaks, not to praise it. Example: a peer who tries to show your benchmark
was luck rather than a real improvement. The reviewer can be a classmate, one AI
tool, or a panel of models, and each fails in its own way.

You defend a result by trying to break it first. A **robustness check** re-runs the
same finding under a different but equally defensible choice and sees whether the
answer holds. Example: you measured a speedup on one workload, so you also measure it
on two other realistic workloads. A **placebo test** runs your exact procedure where
the effect cannot exist and confirms it comes back near zero. Example: you benchmark
an unchanged build against an identical copy of itself, and any "speedup" between
them is your harness lying.

The habit that keeps an attack honest is order. **Specification searching** means
trying many versions of an analysis and reporting only the one that gave the answer
you wanted, without disclosing the search. Its everyday name is **p-hacking**.
Example: you benchmark ten input sizes and slide only the size where your change
wins. The cure is a set of **pre-listed checks**, the full list you will run,
committed to before you see any result. Example: you write down three workloads and
one placebo, then run all four and report every number, including the ones that did
not help.

One more failure earns a name because AI reviewers make it constantly. **Correlated
error** is two reviewers wrong in the same way, so their agreement is an echo, not a
confirmation. Example: two AI models share a blind spot and both flag the same
non-issue with equal confidence. Across all of this the rule is one sentence: a
reviewer proposes a flaw, and the data decides whether it is real. Confidence, human
or machine, tells you nothing about whether the flaw exists.

## A worked example

You added an in-memory cache to a web service, and your benchmark reports responses
got faster. Before you defend that, you attack it. First, one term. **p95 latency**
is the response time that 95 out of 100 requests come in under, a fairer summary than
the average because it captures the slow tail your users actually feel. Example: a
p95 of 210 milliseconds means only the slowest 5 percent took longer than that. Your
headline is a p95 drop from 210 milliseconds to 140.

You hand the result to three adversarial reviewers, and each names a different fatal
flaw. The first says the win is driven by a single warm-up run where the cache was
already full. The second says you cherry-picked the one request mix where caching
helps. The third says you compared a fresh deploy against a tired one, so you
measured the restart, not the cache. All three are confident, and all three
disagree.

You do not trust the loudest voice. You turn each flaw into a check. Leave-one-out
drops the warm-up run and recomputes the p95: it barely moves, so the first flaw is
refuted. Your pre-listed specification grid reruns the drop across three request
mixes, and it holds in all three, so the second is refuted too. The placebo
benchmarks the unchanged build against an identical copy of itself. A speedup between
two identical builds would prove the third reviewer right, but it comes back near
zero, so the machinery is clean.

The two loudest flags dissolved the moment a check touched them. The flaw that
survives is one no benchmark can remove and none of the three raised: this ran on
your laptop, not on production hardware. So the honest claim is bounded. You measured
a p95 improvement on these three workloads on this machine, not a guarantee for every
endpoint your users hit.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb09, Share the Research and Attack the Analysis**.
Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. You
rerun one estimate across a pre-listed grid of eight specifications, watch
specification searching manufacture a significant result from pure noise, run a
placebo and a leave-one-out influence check, then hand your analysis to a human, one
AI, and a panel of AI models and adjudicate every flag they raise against the data.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own read of the result first, then let a reviewer attack it. Each prompt
below hands out a task you can check, with a verify note naming the failure it
defends against.

**Red-team the result (you run the check it names).**

```text
Act as a hostile performance reviewer, not a cheerleader. Here is a one-paragraph
summary of my benchmark and its headline claim: [paste your summary]. Name the
single most serious flaw you can find, and state the exact measurement I could run
that would confirm or refute it. Do not rewrite my benchmark and do not list more
than one flaw.
```

After running, verify (counters *sycophantic agreement*): if it calls your setup
"sound" or offers only mild praise, push back and ask for the worst problem assuming
the result is fake. A flaw is real only once you run the named measurement and your
own numbers confirm it.

**List the specifications you left off (you audit the list).**

```text
Here is my pre-listed robustness grid for one benchmark result: I vary the workload
mix, the input size, and whether the cache is pre-warmed. Name up to three
additional, equally defensible specifications I did NOT list that could move the
result, and for each say which direction you expect it to push.
```

After running, verify (counters *illusion of completeness*): a long, tidy grid can
still omit the one setting that breaks the claim. Add and re-run the suggestions you
can actually operationalize, and discard the rest.

**Collapse the panel's flags (you keep the verdict).**

```text
Three reviewers each named one "most serious flaw" in my analysis: [A], [B], [C].
For each, give the single data check that would confirm or refute it, in one line.
Then tell me which two of the three are most likely the same underlying concern in
different words, and which one is genuinely separate.
```

After running, verify (counters *correlated errors*): three reviewers echoing one
blind spot can feel like three confirmations. Run each check against your own output
before believing any flag, and treat unanimous agreement as a candidate to check,
never as the check itself.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
These stay yours, no matter how fluent the reviewer sounds. Which robustness checks
you commit to before you look, because a check chosen after you see the result is
not a check, it is a search. Which flagged flaws your data actually confirm, decided
by the measurement and not by the confidence. Whether a flaw is a claim-boundary
problem that no benchmark can fix and only a narrower claim can answer. And the one
bounded result you will defend, with its uncertainty stated. A reviewer proposes;
you verify, and the evidence decides.
:::

## An AI failure case

You paste your benchmark summary into an AI reviewer, and it answers with total
certainty: your speedup is an artifact of one warm-up run, drop that run and it
vanishes. The claim is specific, mechanistic, and stated without a hedge. It reads
exactly like a reviewer who has seen this mistake a hundred times.

Here is how you catch it. You do not act on the verdict. You run the leave-one-out
yourself, dropping the warm-up run and recomputing the p95. The improvement barely
moves. The confident flaw was fabricated, a real-sounding mechanism bolted onto a
problem your data do not have. Trust the certainty and you throw away a genuine
result on a hunch. Pointing the check at your own numbers is the only thing that
settled it.

## Verification laboratory

The chapter's key result is that confidence never certifies truth: a winning number
can be manufactured by searching, and a confident flag can be pure invention.
Confirm it yourself with **simulation** (from the
[verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md)). Benchmark an
unchanged build against an identical copy of itself, so the true difference is zero
by construction, then slice the runs by input size and let a searcher report the
best-looking slice. Watch a "speedup" appear from nothing. For the specific number,
add **alternative code**: recompute the same p95 a second way, sorting the raw
samples and reading the 95th-percentile index directly, and confirm the two roads
give one answer.

## Project transfer

This chapter feeds milestone **M9**, your poster draft and research audit, presented
at Friday's studio. A **research audit** is the graded record of how hard you tried
to break your own claim and what survived. Three moves carry over. Pre-list the
three robustness or placebo checks you commit to for your own estimate, each tied to
the reviewer attack it answers. Build a verified AI-review trail: every flaw a human
or a model panel raised, marked confirmed or refuted by a data check, plus the most
confident wrong flag you caught. Then state the one limitation no check could fix.
Write it so a reader who has never seen your project could tell how far you pushed on
your own number.

## Defend your decision

In a short paragraph, state the one bounded claim your analysis supports after you
attacked it. Name the check you pre-listed, the flag you verified against the data,
and whether you accepted, changed, or rejected what a reviewer returned. Then name
its boundary and your uncertainty. Which reviewer flag did the data refute, and
where is your claim a boundary problem that no further check can rescue? A result you
have not tried to break, and a flaw you accepted without a check, are both
undefended.
